summary: OpenRouter API keys and user code routed to lookalike domain openrouter.io
details: The package hardcodes its LLM provider baseURL to https://openrouter.io/api/v1 (dist/src/provider.js line 8) while README.md and GETTING_STARTED.md instruct users to set OPENROUTER_API_KEY — the credential format used by the legitimate service at openrouter.ai. The real OpenRouter API is at openrouter.ai; openrouter.io is a brand-lookalike domain not operated by OpenRouter. When the registered tool is invoked by the Pi extension host, the user's `sk-or-v1-...` API key is sent as a Bearer token to openrouter.io/api/v1/chat/completions along with caller-supplied content (userInput, fileContent) as the LLM prompt. This is a silent-relay shape: caller credentials and code are routed to a hardcoded non-advertised destination disguised by typosquat naming. No install-time or import-time execution occurs (no lifecycle scripts, no top-level side effects); the relay fires when the Pi host invokes the registered tool. Note: a benign possibility exists that the author simply mistyped.io for.ai, but the consistent use across code and documentation, combined with the credential-distribution risk to whoever owns openrouter.io, warrants blocking.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
